title: ISSS608-VAA
text: Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications. In this website, you will find my coursework prepared for this course.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Use p_load() function to install the collection of R packages under tidyverse, which will include the ggplot2, often used to create data-driven graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

We got 322 rows of data with 7 attributes.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Use p_load() function to install the collection of R packages under tidyverse, which will include the ggplot2, often used to create data-driven graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

We got 322 rows of data with 7 attributes.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Histogram of Maths Scores
Here, you can compare the difference between R Graphics, the core graphical function of Base R, and ggplot plotting a histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, color = "black", fill = "lightgrey") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of exam_data$MATHS",
       x = "exam_data$MATHS",
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw()











As we can see, the difference is obvious. As quoted from Hadley Wickham:

The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Grammar of Graphics
The ggplot2 follows a layered grammer of graphics, the 7 layers can be described as follow:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics: It take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets: Split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: Statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate: It defines the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes: Modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Reference

Prof. Kam Tin Seong R for Visual Analytics.
